description: A versatile interjection used to express surprise, realization, regret, or dismissal depending on the tone.

exampleSentences.hindi: ओह, मुझे यह नहीं पता था!

usageContext: Commonly used to acknowledge new information ('Ach so!') or to express a sigh-like dismissal.
pronunciationNotes: The 'ch' is the soft ich-laut or hard ach-laut depending on the preceding vowel; usually a short, breathy exhale.

description: An exclamation of disgust or revulsion towards something unpleasant.

exampleSentences.german: Igitt! Da ist eine Spinne in meiner Suppe!
exampleSentences.english: Yuck! There's a spider in my soup!
exampleSentences.hindi: छी! मेरी सूप में एक मकड़ी है!
छी, इसकी गंध बहुत खराब है!

usageContext: Used when seeing, smelling, or tasting something repulsive.
pronunciationNotes: Short vowels with a sharp emphasis on the second syllable.

description: Used to express mild surprise, a small startle, or a minor mistake.

exampleSentences.german: Huch, das wollte ich nicht umwerfen.
exampleSentences.english: Oops, I didn't mean to knock that over.
exampleSentences.hindi: ओह, मैं इसे गिराना नहीं चाहता था।

usageContext: Often used when someone bumps into something or is surprised by someone entering a room.
pronunciationNotes: A sudden, short burst of air, often with a rising pitch.

description: A filler or interjection used to express hesitation, doubt, or to transition in conversation.

exampleSentences.german: Na ja, es könnte schlimmer sein.
exampleSentences.hindi: खैर, यह और भी बुरा हो सकता था।

exampleSentences.german: Wie war der Film? - Na ja, geht so.
exampleSentences.english: How was the movie? - Well, it was okay.

usageContext: Used to soften a statement or express that a situation is not ideal but acceptable.
pronunciationNotes: The 'na' is often drawn out, followed by a slight pause before 'ja'.

description: The standard German toast used before drinking, usually with beer or wine.

exampleSentences.german: Ein Bier bitte. - Hier, bitte sehr. - Danke, Prost!
exampleSentences.english: A beer please. - Here you go. - Thanks, cheers!
exampleSentences.hindi: एक बीयर देना। - ये लीजिए। - शुक्रिया, चीयर्स!

usageContext: Used in social settings like bars, parties, or dinners when clinking glasses.
pronunciationNotes: The 'r' is often rolled or tapped, and the 'o' is long.

description: An exclamation used when someone makes a minor mistake, trips, or drops something.

exampleSentences.german: Hoppla! Das Glas ist mir fast aus der Hand gerutscht.
exampleSentences.english: Oops! The glass almost slipped out of my hand.
exampleSentences.hindi: ओह! गिलास लगभग मेरे हाथ से फिसल गया था।

exampleSentences.german: Hoppla, ich habe deinen Namen vergessen.
exampleSentences.hindi: अरे, मैं तुम्हारा नाम भूल गया।

usageContext: Used for small physical mishaps or minor social blunders.
pronunciation: Short 'o', with the stress on the first syllable and a light, bouncy tone.
